title: Introduction
text:
Louvain helped us explore the fraud network. But when it comes to formal community assignment, we need algorithms that are deterministic, explainable, and give us precise control. In this lesson, we're diving into two foundational algorithms: Degree Centrality, which counts connections, and Weakly Connected Components, or WCC, which finds connected groups. We'll start by practicing on the Movies graph and then apply what we learn to fraud detection. 

[IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-4-wcc-deep-dive-s264-000-new]
As you can see here, this diagram compares the Louvain, Degree Centrality, and WCC algorithms, highlighting their characteristics and use cases. Each algorithm is represented by a distinct node cluster with labeled characteristics.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to calculate degree centrality with different orientations and weights, explain the Union-Find algorithm underlying WCC, configure WCC with thresholds, seeding, and performance optimizations, and choose between Louvain and WCC based on your requirements.

title: What Degree Centrality Measures
text:
Degree centrality is all about counting how many relationships a node has. By default, it counts outgoing relationships. So, if a node has 5 outgoing connections, its degree is 5. 

[IMAGE: images/degree_centrality.png]
Here, you can see a node with 5 outgoing relationships and three incoming ones, labeled as having a degree of 5. This is the simplest centrality measure—no iteration, no convergence, just counting. But don't let its simplicity fool you; it's incredibly useful.

title: Project Movies and Actors
text: Let's practice degree centrality on the Movies dataset. We'll create a bipartite graph where Actor nodes are connected to Movie nodes. This setup will help us explore degree centrality before applying it to fraud detection.

title: Run Degree Centrality
text: Now, let's count how many movies each actor has appeared in. This query will return the actors and the number of movies they've been in, ordered by the most prolific actors first.

title: Examine results
text: This query should return a table that looks something like this, showing the most prolific actors. The degree score equals the number of movies they've appeared in.

title: Orientation Options
text: You can control the direction in which the algorithm should run using orientation. The default is NATURAL, which counts outgoing relationships. REVERSE counts incoming relationships, and UNDIRECTED counts both forward and back.

title: Counting Incoming Relationships
text: Use REVERSE to count incoming relationships—essentially, how many actors are in each movie. Note that the movies in the underlying dataset have a maximum cast number of 10.

title: Write Degree to Nodes
text: Let's write these degree values back to the database for later use. This will allow us to reference these values in future analyses.

title: Verify the Written Property
text: Now, let's check that the degree property was written correctly. This ensures that our data is accurate and ready for further analysis.

title: Clean Up Actors-Movies Projection
text: Finally, let's drop the actors-movies projection to clean up our workspace. This helps maintain an organized environment for future projects.

title: What WCC Does
text:
Weakly Connected Components, or WCC, groups nodes by connectivity. Two nodes are in the same component if any path connects them, regardless of relationship direction or path length. There's no optimization or density calculation here—just reachability. 

[IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-4-wcc-deep-dive-s277-004-new]
This illustration shows how WCC groups nodes based on connectivity, highlighting how nodes are grouped into components with highlighted yellow edges.

title: Islands and Bridges
text:
Think of nodes as islands and relationships as bridges. WCC answers the question: "Which islands can you walk between?" If any sequence of bridges connects two islands, they're in the same component. 

[IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-4-wcc-deep-dive-s278-001-existing]
Here, you can see seven islands represented as node clusters. Those connected by bridges are labeled as single components, illustrating the concept of reachability.

title: How WCC Works: Union-Find
text:
Under the hood, WCC uses the Union-Find algorithm, also known as Disjoint Set Union. This algorithm has three operations: Make-Set, where each node starts as its own component; Union, which merges two endpoint components when processing a relationship; and Find, which determines which component a node belongs to. 

[IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-4-wcc-deep-dive-s279-005-new]
This flowchart depicts the Union-Find algorithm's operations and how they work together in WCC.

title: Project Actor Collaborations
text: Let's run WCC on actor collaborations. We'll project actor collaborations for WCC, setting up a network where actors are connected based on shared movie appearances.

title: Run WCC
text: Now, let's write the connected components to the graph. This step will help us identify which actors are part of the same collaboration groups.

title: Examine WCC Results
text: Let's fetch the components from the graph to see which actors are grouped together. This will give us insight into the collaboration networks within the dataset.

title: Examining WCC results
text: You should get a table that looks something like this. You'll likely see one giant component containing most actors, which represents the main Hollywood network, plus several smaller isolated groups. This is typical of collaboration networks.

title: Examine Component Distribution
text: Let's see how components are distributed by running the algorithm in stats mode. This will give us a better understanding of the overall structure of the network.

title: Return just one stats fields
text: If you're only interested in the communityDistribution field, you can use YIELD and RETURN to specify just that. This allows you to focus on the most relevant data for your analysis.

title: WCC vs Louvain
text: Let's compare WCC and Louvain. WCC groups nodes based on any path connecting them, while Louvain optimizes for modularity or density. WCC is deterministic and always produces identical results, whereas Louvain can vary between runs. This table summarizes the key differences between the two algorithms.

title: Why Determinism Matters
text:
Determinism is crucial in certain scenarios. Louvain might assign a user to different communities in different runs, but WCC always produces identical results. This consistency is important in fraud investigations, legal scrutiny, and when results must be reproducible for audits. 

[IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-4-wcc-deep-dive-s287-007-new]
This illustration shows the difference in determinism between Louvain and WCC, with examples of how results might vary or remain consistent.

title: WCC Finds Components Based on What You Project
text:
The power of WCC lies in how you define what "connected" means. You can project all relationships to find everyone who's reachable or project only specific relationships to find targeted groups. 

[IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-4-wcc-deep-dive-s288-008-new]
This diagram illustrates how projecting different relationships affects the results of WCC, showing examples of broad versus targeted projections.

title: Weighted WCC with Thresholds
text: Not all connections are equally meaningful. A single shared movie is different from ten shared movies. You can use a threshold to ignore weak connections. For example, with a threshold of 5.0, only connections where actors shared more than two movies are considered. This breaks the giant component into smaller, more meaningful groups of frequent collaborators.

title: Seeded Components
text:
What happens when new data arrives daily? For billion-node graphs, full recomputation is expensive. Seeded WCC lets you build incrementally. The idea is to store previous component IDs and only recompute for new or changed nodes. 

[IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-4-wcc-deep-dive-s291-010-new]
This flowchart illustrates the process of seeded WCC, showing how previous component IDs are used to incrementally update the graph.

title: Seeding: Step 1 — Project with Seed Property
text: The first time we ran WCC on the Movies graph, we wrote the component IDs to the graph. Now, let's include existing component IDs in our projection. This step sets the stage for incremental updates.

title: Seeding: Step 2 — Run WCC with seedProperty
text: Reference the seed property in your WCC call. Nodes with existing component ID values start in those components, while new nodes trigger computation. This approach allows for efficient updates without full recomputation.

title: How Seeding Works
text: When WCC encounters seeded nodes, those with seed values start in their seeded component. New nodes start as singleton components. Union-Find proceeds normally, merging as relationships are processed. If two seeded components connect, they merge using the lower ID. This approach preserves existing structure while integrating new data appropriately.

title: Seeding: Important Caveat
text: Seeding assumes components only grow or merge—never split. If relationships are removed, not just added, seeding can produce incorrect results. In such cases, a full recompute is necessary to ensure accuracy.

title: Performance: Sampled vs Unsampled
text: WCC has two internal strategies: Unsampled, used for directed graphs by default, and Sampled, used for undirected graphs or directed with inverse index. The sampled strategy is faster, using subgraph sampling to reduce work while maintaining correctness.

title: Enabling the Sampled Strategy
text: For directed graphs, add inverseIndexedRelationshipTypes during projection. This allows WCC to traverse relationships in both directions, enabling the optimized algorithm. While the performance gains are minimal on smaller graphs, this is especially important on large, constantly updating graphs.

title: Memory Estimation
text: Before running on production data, it's crucial to estimate memory. If the estimated memory exceeds available heap, GDS will block execution. This step helps you plan accordingly and avoid potential issues.

title: Clean Up
text: Let's drop the actor network projection to clean up our workspace. This ensures that our environment remains organized and ready for future analyses.

title: Transfer: From Movies to Fraud
text: You've practiced on actor collaboration networks. Now, let's apply these algorithms to fraud detection. In this context, actor nodes become user nodes, movie nodes become card, device, or IP nodes, and collaboration components become fraud ring components.

title: When to Use Each Algorithm
text: Here's a quick guide on when to use each algorithm. Degree Centrality is simple, fast, and interpretable, making it great for filtering noise and identifying hubs. WCC is deterministic, explainable, and scalable, ideal for formal grouping and auditable results. Louvain finds dense clusters and is useful for exploration and discovering unknown patterns.

title: The Lesson 5 Strategy
text: In Lesson 5, you'll combine everything you've learned. You'll create fraud-hypothesis relationships, use degree to filter out high-degree noise nodes, run WCC to find connected components of suspects, and label fraud risk by marking users connected to known fraudsters.

title: Summary
text: Let's recap what we've covered. Degree Centrality counts relationships and is essential for filtering noise before community detection. You can use orientation to control direction and weights to measure connection strength. WCC finds connected components and is deterministic and explainable, making it ideal for formal assignment. It uses thresholds to filter weak connections and seeding for incremental processing. Inverse indexing improves performance on directed graphs. Both algorithms are simple by design, but their power comes from what you project and how you configure them. In the next lesson, you'll apply these algorithms to build fraud communities and identify previously unknown fraud risk users.
